query: role of fluoroscopy in ureteroscopic stone retrieval latest guidelines

retrieved_docs.title: Ureteroscopy -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: This activity reviews ureteral anatomy; the indications, contraindications, surgical techniques, and complications of ureteroscopy; evaluation and follow-up care; and preventive strategies. The roles of flexible and semi-rigid ureteroscopes, ureteral access sheaths, and various lasers available for ureteroscopic renal and ureteral lithotripsy and their proper use are discussed. In addition, the use of alpha-blockers in patients to facilitate ureteroscopic procedures is addressed. This activity also highlights the importance of shared decision-making discussions with patients and the roles of the interprofessional team in providing optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: Ureteroscopy -- Technique or Treatment -- Laser Lithotripsy Technique
retrieved_docs.content: The AUA guidelines on stones recommend that all patients with ureteroscopic stone surgery be informed about the potential benefits and availability of 24-hour urine testing and prophylactic medical therapy for nephrolithiasis. [96] Interpretation of 24-hour urine tests has become much easier and is now routinely performed by many urologists, nephrologists, and primary care physicians using published guides. [97]

retrieved_docs.title: Ureterolithiasis -- Treatment / Management -- Tips, advice, and suggestions during ureteroscopy
retrieved_docs.content: A flat-plate KUB X-ray and retrograde pyelogram or ureterogram are recommended immediately before inserting the ureteroscope or ureteral access sheath to visualize internal renal anatomy, stone localization, and orientation during ureteroscopy. A range of ureteral access sheaths, guide wires, baskets, and graspers must be readily available because predicting preoperatively which ones will be required is impossible. The scope must be lubricated during longer cases to facilitate removal and reinsertion. Periodic fluoroscopic checks can help with orientation and ensure that the entire kidney and all calyces are examined.

retrieved_docs.title: Ureteroscopy -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify appropriate indications and contraindications for ureteroscopy in managing urinary tract stones and upper urinary tract lesions. Apply best practices and recommended techniques for performing ureteroscopy. Select the appropriate methods for evaluating potential complications of ureteroscopy. Implement interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to advance ureteroscopy and improve patient outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Double J Placement Methods Comparative Analysis -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The American Urological Association (AUA) guidelines suggest that a double J stent may be omitted after ureteroscopic lithotripsy when "there is no ureteral injury, no anatomical obstacle to fragment clearance and a normal contralateral kidney" but it fails to give any additional guidance on this subject. [2] There is also a wide gap in double J stent usage after ureteroscopy with 40.5% of cases in the Netherlands using stents after ureteroscopic procedures compared to about 93% in the US. [3] A preliminary study presented at the 2018 AUA meeting by Bower and Pareek attempted to create a practical algorithm to help identify situations where stents could reasonably be omitted.  They reported on 250 procedures in which 106 patients were eligible for stent omission based on their algorithm.  Of these 106, 60 went without double J stents, and none of these patients required readmission or additional surgery. Mainly, if a pre-procedural double J stent was in place or an access sheath was not used with a single, uncomplicated renal or distal ureteral stone, a post-procedure double J stent could probably be safely omitted.  Use of an access sheath without pre-procedure stenting would generally require a post-ureteroscopy double J stent. While further prospective studies are needed to confirm the validity of the algorithm, it serves as a useful and valuable starting point for consideration of stent omission after ureteroscopy.

retrieved_docs.title: Ureterolithiasis -- Treatment / Management -- Tips, advice, and suggestions during ureteroscopy
retrieved_docs.content: Double J stents are generally left after ureteroscopic surgery. However, guidelines from the American Urological Association and the European Association of Urology now allow the omission of postoperative stents after uncomplicated ureteroscopies. Uncomplicated ureteroscopy typically entails a unilateral procedure in a healthy patient with a normal contralateral kidney without requiring ureteral access sheaths or balloon ureteral dilation for stones 10 mm or smaller and with no significant residual fragments or ureteral damage.

retrieved_docs.title: Ureterolithiasis -- Treatment / Management -- Ureteroscopy advantages and disadvantages
retrieved_docs.content: However, ureteroscopy is quite invasive, requires a general anesthetic, and can take considerable operating room time for a complex stone, especially in the kidney. This procedure has a significantly higher risk of complications than ESWL, although the overall risk is still relatively low. Performing ureteroscopy may be difficult without a guide wire. In cases where easy ureteroscopic access is not possible, a double J stent may be left, and the procedure rescheduled for a later time, requiring a second surgery. Pre-stenting can facilitate ureteroscopy, but if not performed, postoperative double J stenting is typically required, potentially leading to significant discomfort. For more information and details on ureteroscopy, see StatPearls' companion reference, " Ureteroscopy ."

retrieved_docs.title: Ureteroscopy -- Technique or Treatment -- Laser Lithotripsy Technique
retrieved_docs.content: The availability of 24-hour urine testing for stone prophylaxis should always be discussed with the patient and documented in the medical record, especially for those with high surgical or anesthesia risk. Only highly motivated patients are likely to benefit substantially from prophylactic treatment as it requires long-term compliance. Guidelines recommend discussing the pros and cons of 24-hour urine testing with all urolithiasis patients, especially those who have already undergone ureteroscopic surgery. 24-hour urine testing is particularly recommended in the following situations: [97] [107] [108]

retrieved_docs.title: Ureteroscopy -- Technique or Treatment -- Laser Lithotripsy Technique
retrieved_docs.content: In uncomplicated cases where stones can be easily removed and there is minimal resistance to ureteral dilation or the placement of an access sheath, patients who previously had a double J stent before ureteroscopy may not require a stent after the procedure. [86] Guidelines from the AUA and European Association of Urology now allow the omission of postoperative double J stents after uncomplicated ureteroscopies. [13] [70] [86] [87] Uncomplicated ureteroscopy is defined as follows: [87]

retrieved_docs.title: Ureterolithiasis -- Treatment / Management -- Tips, advice, and suggestions during ureteroscopy
retrieved_docs.content: Double J stents are typically employed in cases involving ureteral access sheaths, impacted stones, prolonged operating times, larger stone size or burden, older patient age, solitary kidneys, ureteral trauma or damage, and heightened intrarenal complexity or complications. Leaving a stent in such situations significantly reduces postoperative complications but can lead to stent discomfort.

retrieved_docs.title: Ureterolithiasis -- Treatment / Management -- Tips, advice, and suggestions during ureteroscopy
retrieved_docs.content: Flexible ureteroscopes are typically used with ureteral access sheaths. The sheath’s width and length should be carefully measured so it is not longer than the scope. The sheath should be placed just distal to the stone or close to the renal pelvis at the UPJ to access the kidney. A safety guide wire is recommended to maintain access, with the wire’s distal end carefully secured.

retrieved_docs.title: Ureteroscopy -- Technique or Treatment -- Laser Lithotripsy Technique
retrieved_docs.content: A  double J ureteral stent may be placed over the remaining safety guidewire and inspected for good curl both within the renal pelvis and distally in the bladder. This decision is up to the individual surgeon but is strongly recommended for patients with ureteral damage on ureteroscopic inspection and those with an increased risk of bleeding, perforation, UTIs, or significant residual fragments. [70] Leaving a stent in such situations significantly reduces postoperative complications but can lead to stent discomfort. [85]

retrieved_docs.title: Ureteroscopy -- Technique or Treatment -- Laser Lithotripsy Technique
retrieved_docs.content: The dual-lumen catheter is the ideal tool for replacing the second guidewire, assuming the safety guidewire is still in place, allowing the ureteral access sheath to be easily replaced. The ureteral access sheath should never be advanced without the dilator in place and only over a guidewire. The ureteral access sheath should never be advanced except with the inner dilator in place. The inner dilator should never be placed or advanced except over a guidewire due to the very significant risk of a perforation of the ureter or renal pelvis. All stone fragments removed should be sent for chemical analysis. [84]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 130
retrieved_docs.content: Ureteral catheterization is the central procedural concept linking a spectrum of endoscopic and imaging‑guided interventions within the urinary tract. It includes cystoscopic catheterization of the ureter, ureteroscopic dilatation, and bilateral or unilateral endoscopic ureteric catheterization performed under fluoroscopic guidance, all of which are mapped to the radiographic category “Other x‑ray of the urinary system.” These catheter‑based techniques are grouped with “Cystoscopy and other transurethral procedures,” under which transurethral excision, drainage, or removal of urinary obstruction is classified. Both the ureteral catheterization and the transurethral obstruction‑removal procedures are direct interventions on the ureter, and they share the child procedure “URETER: INJECTIONS AND IMPLANTATIONS” (Arabic “عملية في الحالب”). Ureteroplication is another direct ureteral operation, reinforcing the ureter as a primary procedural site. The renal pelvis (Czech “ledvinná pánvička”)

retrieved_docs.content: catheterization is the parent concept for a range of ureter‑related endoscopic procedures, including cystoscopic catheterization of the ureter, transurethral cystoscopy, ureteral injections and implantations, ureteroscopic dilatation, and bilateral or unilateral endoscopic ureteric catheterization with fluoroscopic imaging of the upper urinary tract. “Procedures on ureter” are defined as the inverse of transurethral excision, drainage, or removal of urinary obstruction, and the ureter itself is an indirect procedural site within this hierarchy. Ureteroplication directly targets the ureter, while “URETER: INJECTIONS AND IMPLANTATIONS” is classified under “عملية في الحالب” (procedure in the ureter). The group “إجراءات تصوير المسالك البولية” (urinary tract imaging procedures) encompasses urethroscopy (تنظير الإحليل) and endoscopic urinary‑tract procedures such as nephroscopy, electrohydraulic lithotripsy of renal calculi, and renal endoscopy performed through an established nephrostomy

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 203
retrieved_docs.content: Ureteral catheterization is a central procedural category that encompasses a range of endoscopic and imaging‑guided interventions on the ureter and adjacent urinary structures. It includes cystoscopic catheterization of the ureter, which is a specific type within the broader parent group “Cystoscopy and other transurethral procedures,” and is related to the child procedure “Transurethral excision; drainage; or removal of urinary obstruction.” The higher‑level set “Procedures on Ureter” contains ureteral catheterization and the same transurethral excision procedure, indicating their close connection. Derived interventions from ureteral catheterization comprise ureteroscopic dilatation of the ureter and bilateral or unilateral endoscopic ureteric catheterization performed under fluoroscopic guidance; the latter are also mapped from “Other x‑ray of the urinary system.” Anatomically, the ureter serves as an indirect procedure site for ureteral catheterization, while the renal pelvis

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 245
retrieved_docs.content: Ureteral catheterization is a core urologic intervention classified under Procedures on Ureter and includes specific techniques such as Cystoscopic catheterization of ureter, Ureteroscopic dilatation of ureter, and Endoscopic ureteric catheterization with fluoroscopic imaging of the upper urinary tract (both bilateral and unilateral). These endoscopic catheterizations are also mapped to Other x‑ray of the urinary system because they rely on fluoroscopic guidance. The parent category Cystoscopy and other transurethral procedures encompasses ureteral catheterization and contains the child procedure Transurethral excision, drainage, or removal of urinary obstruction, which is simultaneously a type of Procedures on Ureter, illustrating its dual diagnostic‑therapeutic role. URETER: INJECTIONS AND IMPLANTATIONS is a child of ureteral catheterization and of عملية في الحالب (Arabic “procedure in the ureter”), reinforcing its placement within ureter‑focused interventions. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 206
retrieved_docs.content: procedures performed through an established nephrostomy or pyelostomy. Percutaneous nephrostomy tube insertion uses the renal pelvis as an indirect procedural site. Ureteral interventions are centered on ureteral catheterization. Cystoscopic catheterization of the ureter, ureteroscopic dilatation, ureteroplication, and bilateral or unilateral endoscopic ureteric catheterization with fluoroscopic imaging are all specialized applications of this framework. These procedures belong to the broader groups “Cystoscopy and other transurethral procedures,” “Procedures on Ureter,” and the Arabic parent “عملية في الحالب” (procedure in the ureter), with “URETER: INJECTIONS AND IMPLANTATIONS” as a child category. Transurethral excision, drainage, or removal of urinary obstruction is classified under cystoscopic and transurethral procedures, is a child of the ureteral procedural hierarchy, and uses the renal pelvis as an indirect site. It is inversely related to the general “Procedures on Ureter”

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 349
retrieved_docs.content: Nephroscopy and electrohydraulic lithotripsy of renal calculus is classified as a form of endoscopy and endoscopic biopsy of the urinary tract, indicating that it is an endoscopic procedure used to access the urinary system. This technique directly addresses the morphology of حَصاة, the Arabic term for a stone, by allowing visualization and fragmentation of renal calculi during the endoscopic intervention. In parallel, extracorporeal lithotripsy; urinary is a non‑invasive treatment that targets the pathology of حَصاة, using external shock waves to break down urinary stones. Together, these modalities represent complementary approaches to managing stone disease: the endoscopic nephroscopy and electrohydraulic lithotripsy provides direct, intra‑renal access and morphological assessment of the stone, while urinary extracorporeal lithotripsy addresses the underlying stone pathology from outside the body. Both are integral to comprehensive care for patients with renal calculi.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1728
retrieved_docs.content: Extracorporeal lithotripsy; urinary is a non‑invasive therapeutic technique used to address the pathology of حَصاة (urinary stones). By delivering focused shock waves to the stone, the procedure fragments the حَصاة into smaller pieces that can be passed spontaneously, thereby reducing the need for more invasive surgical interventions and improving patient outcomes in the management of urolithiasis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 707
retrieved_docs.content: Ureteroplication is a surgical procedure performed directly on the ureter, targeting this tubular structure to correct or modify its function. Similarly, renal endoscopy conducted through an established nephrostomy or pyelostomy—accompanied by ureteral catheterization and dilation of the ureter—also involves direct manipulation of the ureter. Both interventions are urologic techniques that focus on the ureter, highlighting its central role in procedures aimed at diagnosing, treating, or managing ureteral abnormalities and related renal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 56
retrieved_docs.content: its systemic relevance. Overall, the knowledge base illustrates how abdominal and peritoneal surgical interventions, renal endoscopic and lithotripsy procedures, associated diseases, and a spectrum of diagnostic and therapeutic imaging techniques are interrelated, supporting comprehensive patient assessment and multidisciplinary care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1954
retrieved_docs.content: Endoscopic ureteric catheterization with fluoroscopic imaging of the upper urinary tract, whether performed bilaterally or unilaterally, is classified as an “other x‑ray of the urinary system.” This categorization indicates that both the bilateral and unilateral endoscopic catheterization procedures use fluoroscopic guidance to visualize the upper urinary tract and are considered radiologic examinations within urinary system imaging. Recognizing these procedures as a type of urinary system x‑ray helps clinicians understand their diagnostic and therapeutic role in urologic care, guiding appropriate use of imaging resources and informing patient management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 836
retrieved_docs.content: Ureteral catheterization serves as a central procedural category within endourology. Cystoscopic catheterization of ureter is classified as a ureteral catheterization technique. The broader grouping “Cystoscopy and other transurethral procedures” is linked to ureteral catheterization as its parent category and includes as a child the specific activity of transurethral excision, drainage, or removal of urinary obstruction. Ureteroscopic dilatation of ureter is derived primarily from the ureteral catheterization concept, indicating that dilation is often performed using ureteroscopic catheter techniques. Both bilateral and unilateral endoscopic ureteric catheterization performed with fluoroscopic imaging of the upper urinary tract are also primarily mapped from ureteral catheterization, underscoring their reliance on catheter‑based endoscopic access. In addition, these bilateral and unilateral fluoroscopic catheterizations are associated with other x‑ray imaging of the urinary system, reflecting their role in radiographic assessment during the procedure. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how various cystoscopic, ureteroscopic, and fluoroscopic catheterization procedures are organized under the umbrella of ureteral catheterization and how they interrelate with transurethral therapeutic actions and diagnostic imaging of the urinary tract.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2478
retrieved_docs.content: Nephroscopy and electrohydraulic lithotripsy of renal calculus and renal endoscopy through established nephrostomy or pyelostomy with ureteral catheterization and dilation of ureter are both classified as forms of endoscopy and endoscopic biopsy of the urinary tract. These specialized procedures represent specific techniques used to visualize, fragment, and remove renal calculi and to access the urinary system via established nephrostomy or pyelostomy, including catheterization and dilation of the ureter. By falling under the broader category of urinary tract endoscopy and biopsy, they illustrate how targeted endoscopic interventions are integral to urologic diagnosis and treatment of renal stones and ureteral pathology.

The ureter serves as an indirect procedure site for ureteral catheterization, whereas ureteroplication is a direct ureteral procedure. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 451
retrieved_docs.content: Ureteral catheterization is a core category of procedures performed on the ureter, encompassing a range of endoscopic and imaging‑guided techniques. Specific interventions that are classified as ureteral catheterization include cystoscopic catheterization of ureter, ureteroscopic dilatation of ureter, and endoscopic ureteric catheterization with fluoroscopic imaging of the upper urinary tract (both bilateral and unilateral). These procedures are also linked to “URETER: INJECTIONS AND IMPLANTATIONS,” which is recognized as a sub‑type of ureteral catheterization and is described in Arabic as عملية في الحالب. Ureteral catheterization itself is a subtype of the broader “Procedures on Ureter” hierarchy, and it is further situated within the larger domain of transurethral excision, drainage, or removal of urinary obstruction.
